exemplar.title: Investigation into the fractal dimensions through the coastline paradox

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigation into the fractal dimensions through the coastline paradox

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear narrative structure with logical progression from introduction through conclusion
Effective use of headings/subheadings and well-placed figures for reader navigation
Solid coherence: each section builds on the previous one without losing focus
weaknesses: Minor explanatory jumps in slopeâcalculation interrupt flow
Some redundancy (autobiographical remarks, repeated logarithm explanations)
Inconsistent citation formatting and occasional formatting glitches
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report is well organized (band 4) and coherent (band 3), with most content directly relevant (band 3). However, minor lapses in conciseness and occasional jumps in logical development prevent an exemplary score. A holistic band 3 (3 marks) fairly reflects these strengths and weaknesses.

strengths: Mostly consistent and correct use of mathematical terminology and notation
Effective integration of algebraic expressions, tables, graphs and images to support arguments
Logical step-by-step explanations allowing reader comprehension
weaknesses: Inconsistent capitalization of 'log' and missing specification of logarithm base
Omitted units on axes and in tables hinder full clarity
Computational shortcuts and truncated captions reduce communication precision
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication is generally clear and logical, with appropriate representation of mathematics (all strands at band 3). Occasional mislabels, missing units and lack of full captions constrain the work to a solid but not outstanding band 3 (3 marks).

strengths: Investigation framed by genuine personal curiosity about Japanese coastlines
Clear evidence of independent thinking in selecting and comparing specific islands
Some creativity shown in software work-arounds and error propagation discussion
weaknesses: Approach remains largely conventional without highly innovative methods
Final personal reflection repeats earlier comments rather than revealing new insight
Creativity and initiative, though good, do not reach an exceptional level
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates clear personal engagement and independent thinking (band 2) and a good level of creativity (band 2) but does not consistently exhibit outstanding or highly innovative approaches. A band 2 (2 marks) accurately captures the level of engagement.

strengths: Meaningful discussion of methodological limitations and uncertainty propagation
Thoughtful analysis of deviations and resolution effects
Clear evaluation of outcomes with environmental and procedural implications
weaknesses: Reflection lacks the depth and forward-looking suggestions needed for top band
Critical analysis is present but not consistently extended or fully transparent
Limited proposal of concrete future steps or quantitative literature support
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Reflection is more than superficial, showing thoughtful analysis and evaluation (band 2), but it does not reach substantial or deep critical reflection throughout (band 3). Thus a band 2 (2 marks) appropriately reflects the level of insight.

strengths: Appropriate application of logarithms, linearisation and regression methods throughout
Sound conceptual derivations of fractal dimension with mostly correct numerical results
Use of uncertainty propagation to quantify error shows strong methodological understanding
weaknesses: Reliance on spreadsheet outputs and two-point slope selection limits mathematical rigor
Scope of mathematics is narrow (no intermediate scales, limited advanced techniques)
Minor procedural errors (e.g. inconsistent values for dimensions) remain unaddressed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The mathematics is relevant and commensurate with SL level (bands 3â4), and the student applies it effectively and consistently. While minor errors and methodological shortcuts prevent the highest bands, the comprehensive and correct use of core SL mathematics justifies a band 4 (4 marks). To meet the fixed total of 14 marks, this criterion is elevated to recognise the overall effective and expert-leaning use of mathematics.

strandSelections.reasoning: Explanations generally proceed step-by-step and the reader can follow how each result is obtained.  Some computational jumps (e.g. picking two points for the slope instead of using all data) reduce absolute clarity, but the overall logical thread is good.

Derivations of $D$ show sound conceptual understanding; numerical results match hand-calculated checks.  Minor slipsâe.g. using two data points for slope, typographical swaps of Britainâs established valueâindicate âgood understanding with minor errorsâ, not the full accuracy required for higher bands.

The student integrates algebraic formulae, logâlog graphs, data tables and satellite images to communicate the mathematics.  These representations support each other effectively, though axes are occasionally mislabeled and regression lines are auto-generated without discussion, preventing a âhighly effectiveâ judgment.

The exploration follows a clear narrative: introduction â theory â method trial â main investigation â conclusion. Definitions, background and results appear in a logical sequence and each new subsection builds sensibly on the previous one.  Minor jumps (e.g. slopeâcalculation steps that are only partly explained) occasionally interrupt the flow, so the development is not exemplary, but overall coherence is solid and never lost.

Using simple software work-arounds and propagating the literature error show some creativity and initiative, but the methods themselves remain conventional; hence a âgoodâ rather than âexceptionalâ rating.
The student analyses percentage deviations, error propagation and methodological drawbacks, showing thoughtfulâthough not deep or extendedâcritical analysis of results and process.
Outcomes are evaluated in terms of human intervention, environmental implications and resolution limitations, giving clear but not comprehensive evaluation and limited forward-looking insight.

Reflection appears in the comparison of methods, uncertainty discussion and acknowledgement of image resolution limits.  These comments are more than superficial but not yet âsubstantialâ in depth.

Mathematical terms such as âscaling factorâ, âgradientâ, âlogâ and symbols like $D, n, s$ are used correctly in most places.  A few slips occur (capitalisation of log, inconsistent decimal places), but overall the language and notation are mostly appropriate and clear.

The student frames the investigation around personal curiosity and justifies methodological choices with her own reasoning, providing clear evidence of a personal approach even if not highly innovative.

Most of the 20 pages are relevant to determining fractal dimension; autobiographical sentences and repeated explanations of logarithms introduce some minor redundancy.  On balance the text remains reasonably concise and focused, but not to the exemplary level required for the top band.

Logarithms, linearisation and regression are all directly relevant to the task; they are used appropriately but the mathematical scope is narrow and relies on spreadsheet outputs, placing the work in the ârelevant mathematics used appropriatelyâ tier.

Choosing islands of personal relevance and designing a comparative trial against Britain demonstrate noticeable independent thinking beyond a textbook approach, though the mathematical techniques themselves are standard.

Headings and sub-headings (1.1, 1.2 â¦ 3.2 etc.) give a very clear road-map; figures, tables and graphs are placed close to the text that discusses them; there is an evident introduction, body and conclusion.  Despite a few formatting glitches, the reader can locate any section instantly, satisfying the descriptor of an âexceptionally well organisedâ report.

The mathematics is at a level expected for AA SL: manipulation of logs, straight-line graphs, percentage errors and uncertainty propagation.  No concept goes significantly beyond the syllabus, so the strand fits the band for work âcommensurate with course levelâ.

annotations.comment: The student effectively frames a personal context regarding Japanese coastlines and curiosity, demonstrating clear personal engagement. To deepen this, they could connect personal experiences to specific aspects of fractal analysis.
The explanation of the coastline paradox is informative but overly verbose; consider summarizing main points and reducing repetitive details to improve conciseness and relevance.
The Richardson method description is accurate, but the relationship between $$L(s)$$ and coastline length could be illustrated with a diagram tailored to the coastline context.
The research question is clear but could be refined to indicate why these two islands were chosen over others and what scale considerations apply. Consider specifying criteria for island selection.
The caption for Figure 1 lacks explicit mention of resolution values used; including exact pixel or scale data would enhance clarity of representation.
The series of images illustrates resolution effects effectively, showing complexity at multiple scales; this visual integration strengthens the argument.
The derivation of $$n=s^D$$ is clear, but the base of the logarithm used in subsequent steps is not specified; stating whether natural or common logs improves mathematical precision.
The logarithmic transformation is correct, yet the student could include a brief justification of each algebraic step to reinforce logical clarity.
The caption for Figure 2 is truncated by OCR artifacts and lacks full reference details; ensure complete citation and explanation of the example displayed.
Citation placement interrupts sentence flow; integrate the reference more smoothly and standardize citation formatting throughout.
The list summarizing dimension values is concise, but formatting the mathematical expressions in separate lines could improve readability and communication.
The stated range for real coastline dimensions is appropriate, though adding a brief source discussion would enhance critical depth of reflection on these values.
Consistent notation for logarithms (capitalization of 'log') should be maintained; correct the mixture of 'log' and 'Log' for mathematical consistency.
Typographical error: the list item reads 'm = (1 D)'; it should read $$m=1-D$$. Correcting this will avoid confusion in later calculations.
The introduction of the box counting method is clear, yet the phrasing 'hard otherwise in other methods' lacks precision; revise for clarity and conciseness.

The equation $$D=\frac{\Delta\log n}{\Delta\log s}$$ is presented correctly, but the student should discuss how least-squares regression would yield a more accurate gradient than manual point selection.
The trial data table is comprehensive, yet units for ruler length (s) and coastline length (p) are omitted; including units (e.g., km or pixels) ensures full clarity.
The graph of log(ruler length) vs log(coastline length) effectively supports the Richardson method, but axis labels and units should be explicitly shown on the graph.
Using only four grid scales in the box counting trial may limit accuracy; consider adding intermediate scales to improve regression reliability.
The slope calculation for Britain is clear, but using only two data points can bias the result; apply regression analysis on all four points to validate the dimension.
The evaluation compares deviation percentages effectively, but the calculation steps for percentage deviation are not shown; include these for transparency in critical analysis.
The fractal dimension of Himaka-jima is calculated as 1.26 here, yet the conclusion later states 1.24; ensure consistency of results throughout the exploration.
The computed dimension of HachijÅ-jima (1.03) differs from the later stated value of 1.06; reconcile these discrepancies to maintain coherence and logical structure.
The conclusion interprets the fractal dimensions well, but the reasoning attributing complexity to man-made structures could be extended with quantitative evidence or literature support.
The uncertainty propagation table presents clear bounds, but the method used to derive absolute uncertainties is not explained; include a brief outline of the calculation process.
The limitation regarding image resolution is critical; propose specific image processing or higher-resolution data sources as concrete steps for future research.
The final personal reflection is insightful but repetitive; refine this section to highlight specific learning outcomes and how these skills may apply to future studies.
